item.title: When emigration helps bad rulers survive
item.description: A new book finds links between mass migration and democratic backsliding worldwide

item.title: Another Chinese Politburo Member Falls
item.description: Ma Xingrui is the latest official ensnared by anti-corruption purges.

item.title: Will Trump Attack or TACO?
item.description: T-minus four hours until either Tehran accepts a cease-fire deal or the United States attacks Iran’s civilian infrastructure.

item.title: The crew of Artemis II is returning to a planet they have cheered up
item.description: The mission has recaptured some of the enthusiasm of early lunar flights

item.title: The Iran War Is Exposing Iraq’s Weaknesses
item.description: Baghdad doesn’t control much of its own territory.

item.title: Meet the four Democratic tribes
item.description: What 19,000 survey respondents reveal about rival factions on the left

item.title: Bye, bye to the Trump trades
item.description: Markets are calling time on many Donald-adjacent bets

item.title: A month into the war, Iran shows no sign of running out of missiles
item.description: Israel seems to have overestimated the damage done to Iran’s missile programme

item.title: Why Energy Has Become a Foreign-Policy Weapon
item.description: Iran’s blockade of the Strait of Hormuz is a reminder of how everything is fair game in an era of great-power competition.

item.title: Middle East Dispatch newsletter: A terrifying wait
item.description: Gregg Carlstrom, our Middle East correspondent, on Donald Trump’s blood-curdling threat to erase Iran

item.title: Trump Is Attacking Iranians, Not Just Iran
item.description: The destruction of infrastructure is killing ordinary people.

item.title: Global democracy is in better shape than you think
item.description: EIU’s annual index suggests an end to the democracy recession

item.title: A wary rapprochement between India and China
item.description: New Indian investment rules are a cautious start

item.title: The Coming Inflation-Deflation Whipsaw
item.description: Other than the pandemic-era supply-chain disruptions, business leaders and investors have not had occasion to think much about either inflation or deflation. But that needs to change, because we are now facing both an immediate threat of higher prices and a longer-term prospect of rapidly falling labor, goods, and services costs.

item.title: The war in the Gulf could cause a global food shock
item.description: Soaring prices for fertiliser and fuel are sowing panic among farmers

item.title: The Road to De-Escalation With Iran
item.description: Around the world, cash-strapped governments and people already struggling with poverty and precarity are asking with increasing urgency: Is there an off-ramp for the latest conflict in the Middle East? There is, but it requires taking economic incentives seriously.

item.title: Preventing an Iranian Bomb Is Only Getting Harder
item.description: A weaker, angrier, more suspicious regime with a less cautious supreme leader and leverage over Hormuz. What could go wrong?

item.title: Israel’s Buffer-Zone Fallacy
item.description: In an era when ballistic missiles, drones, and other projectiles can travel long distances to hit targets with growing accuracy, the idea of a protective buffer zone is not only faulty; it is complete nonsense. Yet this is precisely the rationale that Israel has offered for its occupation of southern Lebanon.

item.title: The War Will End With a Hormuz Toll Booth
item.description: Iran will likely control the waterway. The question is whether diplomats find a way of making that workable.

item.title: The Gulf’s Wartime Unity Is Unraveling
item.description: Some states want escalation; others are pushing for restraint.

item.title: How Women Succeed in Male-Dominated Fields
item.description: Discussions about gender and work often morph into cultural clashes, driven by fear, ideology, or frustration. But recent empirical research suggests that having female peers and professors can help women succeed in the workplace without hurting men.

item.title: Will the BJP Gain a Foothold in Key Indian States in Upcoming Assembly Elections?
item.description: A largely Hindi-belt party, the BJP is looking to break into the strongholds of regional parties in West Bengal, Kerala and Tamil Nadu.

item.title: The EU is Forging a ‘Hedging Alliance’ With Indo-Pacific Middle Powers
item.description: By integrating defense industries and geoeconomic statecraft, Europe and its Indo-Pacific partners are jointly building risk-diversifying mechanisms to navigate the U.S.-China-Russia great power competition.

item.title: Trump’s New Cyber Strategy Is Catnip for Beijing
item.description: Washington thinks offensive cyber power will restore deterrence. It’s mistaken.

item.title: The Real AI Race
item.description: While the invention of general-purpose technologies creates new opportunities, it is their diffusion across industries and economies that drives economic transformation. Early evidence suggests that AI is likely to follow a similar trajectory.

item.title: Southeast Asia’s Coming Food Crisis
item.description: Myanmar will be hit worst.

item.title: What the Deaths of Indonesia’s Peacekeepers Mean for Its Foreign Policy
item.description: The deaths and injuries serve as a stark reminder of the risks of Jakarta becoming more closely involved in a volatile region.

item.title: Vietnam Communist Party Chief To Lam Appointed as State President
item.description: The decision marks a break with the collective decision-making model that has prevailed for most of the country's recent history.

item.title: America Has Lost the Arab World
item.description: Wars in Gaza, Iran, and elsewhere have sunk Washington’s reputation—maybe for good.

item.title: A Flawed Formula for Peace in Ukraine
item.description: Trump can’t end a war with a real estate transaction.

item.title: America and China Can Make AI Safer
item.description: Cooperation is necessary—and possible.

item.title: Rights Groups File Genocide Complaint Against Myanmar’s Min Aung Hlaing
item.description: The complaint was accepted by the Attorney General's Office just days after the general was "elected" president by the military-dominated parliament.

item.title: Iran, U.S. Reject Cease-Fire Proposals
item.description: Trump has given Tehran 24 hours to agree to a peace deal and reopen the Strait of Hormuz or else face massive strikes.

item.title: Iran and America are spinning stories about the size of their victories
item.description: The bombast obscures the scale of the damage the war is doing

item.title: As Iran’s civilian economy crumbles, its military economy grows stronger
item.description: War is splitting the country in two

item.title: Beijing’s Foreign Influence Tactics, Hidden in Plain Sight
item.description: New reports document the scope and sophistication of the CCP's influence operations.

item.title: Why Cheng Li-wun’s Visit to China Matters
item.description: Cheng Li-wun’s visit signals that Beijing is still seeking to influence Taiwan’s political trajectory through selective engagement with the opposition KMT.

item.title: Is Economic Forecasting Still Possible?
item.description: Much of the forecasting profession—and the economic theory that underpins it—still defaults to single baselines that treat the future as a probabilistic replica of the past. But when the structure of the economy changes in unforeseeable ways, as it is now, forecasters must acknowledge that many futures are possible.

item.title: Pakistan’s Search for Diplomatic Relevance Amid the Iran War
item.description: Is Pakistan’s mediation effort a diplomatic breakthrough or an act of geopolitical opportunism?

item.title: Horrors in Sudan Highlight Deterioration of Western Diplomatic Corps
item.description: Decades of neglect have hollowed out expertise and experience.

item.title: Japan Must Stand Up for Peace and Nuclear Disarmament
item.description: At a time of growing global disorder, responsible and diplomatic behavior is in alarmingly short supply.

item.title: Taiwan and the Formosa Disaster in Vietnam: The Sound of Silence
item.description: Ten years after a toxic waste dump poisoned Vietnam’s coast, the Taiwanese company responsible, both governments, and even the victims themselves have maintained a stunning silence.

item.title: Can India Become the World’s Innovation Capital?
item.description: As supply chains fragment, AI rewrites the rules of competition, and geopolitical volatility intensifies, companies are searching for stable, scalable, innovation‑rich environments. India can provide what they need, and with the right enabling policies, it can transform its advantages into a lasting global lead.

item.title: Trump’s Rhetorical Terror
item.description: Violent language has become a consistent and completely counterproductive part of Donald Trump’s foreign policy.

item.title: The strange, multicultural slang of Toronto’s teenagers
item.description: London rappers have an outsize influence

item.title: As more states legalise gambling, what next for Las Vegas?
item.description: Visitor numbers are down. But legalisation presents new opportunities

item.title: A Bad Deal Today Means a Bigger War Tomorrow
item.description: Iran has given the international community little reason to trust it since the 1979 revolution that led to the establishment of the Islamic Republic. Negotiations to end the war today would be dangerously premature, naive, and likely to produce an outcome that all but guarantees another war.

item.title: Taiwan and China are preparing for a summit, of sorts
item.description: A trip to the mainland by the KMT’s leader exposes a rift in the party

item.title: Remembering Vietnam’s Worst Environmental Disaster – and the Ongoing Cover-up
item.description: It’s bad enough that the company at fault considers the issue closed, but the Vietnamese government has effectively sided with a foreign corporation – and arrested those who dared to protest.

item.title: America’s Currency Is the Global South’s Problem
item.description: While the US dollar will not retain its preeminent status forever, countries in the Global South cannot afford to wait out its dominance. With a strategic approach, they can reduce their dependence on the dollar, thereby mitigating the associated risks and fostering a more balanced and resilient global financial landscape.

item.title: How far do the European Union’s military ambitions go?
item.description: An interview with the EU’s top military officer

item.title: Free Trade Is Dead in Washington
item.description: Trumpian chaos conceals a wider bipartisan shift aimed at China.

item.title: Vietnam War Redux?
item.description: In an echo of history, Iran hints at a war of attrition with the U.S. and its allies.

item.title: China’s Absence Draws America Deeper Into Risky Wars
item.description: The lack of a counterweight encourages intervention and overreach.

item.title: Democrats Already Have an Affordability Agenda
item.description: They should reclaim key parts of Joe Biden’s record, rather than run from it.

item.title: Bombing for Freedom
item.description: The idea that bombing civilians will create the conditions for “regime change” is more than a century old. It has never worked, and it won’t work in Iran, because when people are expending all their energy trying to make it to the next day, they don’t have any left over for rebellion.

item.title: The Mispricing of War
item.description: The US-Israeli campaign against Iran is reminding everyone that war is one of the most globally subsidized of human activities. Those who initiate it rarely bear the full costs, which tend to be displaced across borders, markets, and time, making decisions to initiate hostilities more likely.

item.title: India’s Stranded Seafarers Hope New Delhi Can Save Them
item.description: The closure of the Strait of Hormuz has left thousands of Indian sailors at risk.

item.title: India’s 59-Year Maoist Insurgency Collapses
item.description: The government appears to have met its March 31, 2026, deadline for ending the Maoist insurgency. But it is too early to celebrate.

item.title: Sing, O Muse, of the Rage of Donald Trump!
item.description: The war in Iran has all the elements of a Greek tragedy.

item.title: The Iran Shock
item.description: The dangerous allure of energy autarky.

item.title: Avoiding the Next Gulf War
item.description: How America’s allies in the region can get out of the cross hairs.

item.title: Europe Is Stuck With America
item.description: Economic ties will be hard to unwind.

item.title: The South American petro-state profiting from the Iran war
item.description: The boom is raising the risk that oil money devours the economy

item.description: Donald Trump is furious at their refusal to help his war against Iran

item.title: The tumbling rupee is a big problem for Narendra Modi
item.description: India’s prime minister knows the risks of a weak currency

item.description: Western fast-food brands are expanding into the countryside

item.description: Governments’ borrowing costs are being pulled in opposite directions

item.description: Charlotte Howard, our New York bureau chief, on how to judge the president’s failures

item.description: The White House wants to portray Iran’s military as defeated, but U.S. intelligence shows otherwise.

item.title: Why Trump’s Speech Was So Worrying
item.description: Certain outcomes to the U.S. war in Iran now seem more likely than ever.

item.title: A captive American in Iran could lead to further escalation
item.description: It could prompt Donald Trump to follow through on his worst threats

item.title: In Defense of the Hive Mind
item.description: Vince Gilligan’s “Pluribus” asks whether an AI-led society would really be that bad.

item.title: Iran’s Zarif Wants a Bilateral Reset With U.S. That the Gulf Can’t Accept
item.description: Former FM’s terms for ending the war leave out the states that absorbed the most damage.

item.title: Russia’s River of Consciousness
item.description: An undercover journalist travels the Volga to catch a glimpse of life under Putin.

item.title: Two Takes on Modern France
item.description: Plus, more international fiction releases in April.

item.title: What in the World?
item.description: Test yourself on the week of March 28: Energy markets react to the Iran war, Russia expels a diplomat, and the Afghanistan-Pakistan conflict intensifies.

item.title: How the India-Myanmar Border in the Northeast is Being Misread
item.description: The border is often spoken of as if it already exists in a fixed and continuous form, as if the line has been fully drawn and only needs to be defended. The reality is different.

item.title: Why Jet Fuel Is the Real Harbinger of the Energy Crisis
item.description: The Iran war is torching the products that make the world go around.

item.title: The Lost Children of Minab
item.description: It has been one month since missiles struck a school in the Iranian port town.

item.title: Pakistan’s Peacemaking Is a Setback for India
item.description: As Islamabad positions itself as a mediator in the Iran war, New Delhi is sidelined.

item.title: Revisiting Donald Trump and Kim Jong Un’s Last Meeting
item.description: The 2019 snap summit at the DMZ was a mesmerizing reality show, for which there had been no preparation – but it almost worked.

item.title: Iran Is the Dumb, Disastrous Remake of Desert Storm
item.description: Trump is trying to rerun the first Gulf War, badly, in an international system that won’t allow it.

item.title: TSMC’s Kumamoto Fab Upgrade: A Security-Driven Reconfiguration of Indo-Pacific Chip Competition
item.description: The move not only strengthens Japan’s position in the semiconductor sector, but also shows that chip competition is increasingly being shaped by geopolitics rather than market logic.

item.title: Why Australia Should Not Participate in a Trump-Led Invasion of Iran
item.description: Iran is not Iraq circa 2003.

item.title: When Feminist Speech Disappears Online, Chinese Women Find New Ways to Speak
item.description: Post-censorship feminism offers insight into how activism adapts when public speech becomes risky.

item.title: Beijing Is Racing to Get Concessions From Taiwan’s KMT
item.description: Cheng Li-wun is the party’s most pro-Beijing chair in a decade – and China is rushing to take advantage.

item.title: Australia’s EU Critical Minerals Deal Is No Quick Fix for Its China Dependence
item.description: Europe can provide capital, technology and market access, but it cannot quickly replace China’s unmatched role as a buyer, processor and integrated industrial ecosystem.

item.title: Why the Aral Sea’s Restoration Matters Beyond Central Asia
item.description: The Aral Sea’s story demonstrates how human actions can profoundly alter natural systems, yet it also shows that determined cooperation can begin to repair environmental damage that once seemed irreversible.

item.title: The Notion of the Chinese Nation: How Beijing’s New Ethnic Law Pushes Taiwan Further Away
item.description: Contrasting approaches to language and identity highlight a widening divide in how each side conceptualizes nationhood – part of the political and societal gap across the Taiwan Strait.

item.title: Tragedy Is Reborn as Hope
item.description: As the world confronts the twin crises of democratic backsliding and climate change, a new sense of possibility has emerged from tragedy. US President Donald Trump has shown that the person holding political power does, in fact, matter, while the senseless war in the Middle East has renewed hope for completing the energy transition.

item.title: The Energy Shock Hits Latin America
item.description: The Iran war has led the region’s leaders to recalibrate their economic policies.

item.title: Iran on the Edge of Breakdown
item.description: The sustained US-Israeli attacks on Iranian infrastructure have left the country’s legal system without functional enforcement mechanisms. Regardless of whether the Islamic Republic collapses or endures, the country is likely to face a prolonged period of instability.

item.title: Why Iran Is Beating America
item.description: The “asymmetric cost” model—a war the US starts will ultimately cost the other side far more—has proven vital to sustain the illusion of American invincibility and to limit domestic political resistance to US military adventurism. Now, Iran has broken it.

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: How China hopes to win from the war
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed our cover

item.title: Is Financial Deregulation Under Trump Going Too Far?
item.description: Although the Trump administration is rightly rethinking the heavy-handed banking measures implemented after the 2008 financial crisis, it is also slashing staff at key regulatory agencies, reducing capital requirements, and pushing through other changes. No matter how you slice it, the odds of a bad ending have risen.

item.title: When Fools Go to War
item.description: One need only look past the moral and strategic differences between Iran and Ukraine to see that both are facing similar situations. Both have been attacked by larger powers whose institutional decline has produced regimes that failed to anticipate what they were setting into motion.

item.title: The Energy Transition Has Its Own Strait of Hormuz
item.description: The clean-energy transition is often framed as a way to escape the strategic chokepoints and maritime vulnerabilities that have long defined the fossil-fuel industry. But a system built on renewables introduces new risks, as critical bottlenecks shift to refining, processing, and mineral-supply chains.

item.title: How China Reinvented the BRI
item.description: Western tariffs accelerated its transformation into a sophisticated extension of China’s industrial policy.

item.description: A deal Tehran could take.

item.description: An Israeli assault is pushing the fragile country to the brink.

item.description: A deal Tehran could take.

item.title: Ten Years After the ADHOC 5, Cambodia’s Human Rights Defenders Are Still Paying the Price
item.description: The international human rights system depends on the work of local defenders, but offers them little protection once they become politically inconvenient.

item.title: What’s Next for the Iran War?
item.description: A prime-time Trump speech offered few clues.

item.title: Pam Bondi’s loyalty only took her so far
item.description: The president wants the impossible from his attorney-general

item.description: U.S. operations in Iran risk draining limited U.S. stocks.

item.description: Trump has demanded that other countries bear the responsibility of “taking” the strait.

item.description: The Iran war has punctured—though not completely popped—the region’s tech bubble.

item.description: Blocked fertilizer shipments plus La Niña spell trouble for farmers around the world.

item.title: U.S.-South Korea Relations Are at Breaking Point
item.description: he Iran war has confirmed how little Washington cares for its ally’s welfare.

item.title: How Hezbollah Stands to Benefit From an Israeli Invasion
item.description: A renewed occupation could play to the guerrilla group’s strengths.

item.title: Why India Should Leverage BRICS to Call for Ceasefire in West Asia
item.description: What makes BRICS silence on the war in West Asia particularly deplorable is that two fellow BRICS members -- Iran and the UAE -- are directly caught in the war.

item.title: Forgotten Faces in the Contested South China Sea
item.description: Vietnamese fishers, especially those who have faced physical violence from Chinese forces, suffer in silence.

item.title: China and African Sovereignty: Beyond the Digital Silk Road Narrative
item.description: African states have considerable agency to shape their own digital governance and are not simply accepting models imposed on them by China or other external actors.

item.title: Tajikistan’s Households Spend More Than They Earn
item.description: The gap is narrow, but it leaves families with little room to absorb new shocks, including those now emerging from the war in Iran.

item.description: Research shows that some people could benefit

item.description: Despite the positive optics of punching above its weight in diplomacy, Pakistan has become weaker over time.

item.title: Mongolia’s Schools Have a Violence Problem That Goes Far Deeper Than Viral Videos
item.description: When victimized students were asked where they turned for help, 44.5 percent said they searched the internet, while only 2.9 percent went to a friend and fewer than 3 percent contacted the police.

item.title: Amid Iran War Energy Crunch, Taiwan Turns Back Toward Nuclear Energy
item.description: With energy security back in the spotlight, the Lai administration has scrapped a longstanding plank of the DPP platform: opposing nuclear power.

item.title: Outsource AI Risk to the Right People
item.description: Nuclear history shows the importance of keeping skeptics in the room when thinking about safety

item.title: Europe’s Untapped Arsenal
item.description: Ukraine has forged the defense industry the continent desperately needs.

item.title: Why China’s ‘Rare Earth Card’ Is Unlikely to Become a Lasting Geopolitical Lever
item.description: China’s rare earth advantage does not equal strategic supremacy.

item.title: The Economic Impact of Mexico’s Autocratic Drift
item.description: Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum suffered a rare legislative setback in March, one of several early signs that support for her and her predecessor’s political project may be softening. The narrative that Mexico could bend its democratic rules without weakening its economic foundations is now beginning to unravel.

item.title: South Korea’s Moment to Lead on Crimes Against Humanity
item.description: South Korea has a chance to lead in defining the scope, standards, and ambition of a new U.N. convention on crimes against humanity.

item.title: Japan, France Elevate Economic Security Amid Energy Shock
item.description: Their partnership is deepening as the Hormuz crisis drives cooperation on critical minerals, nuclear energy, and supply chain resilience.

item.title: African Clean-Energy Sovereignty Can’t Wait
item.description: Renewable energy is the only way that African economies can escape permanent dependence on foreign imported fuels, currencies, external creditors, and the disastrous effects of wars in other parts of the world. More than a climate measure, it is the foundation for sovereignty and long-term economic stability.

item.title: The China-Pakistan Iran Peace Plan: All Words, No Commitment
item.description: China’s “five-point proposal” is structured to avoid friction or controversy. That’s not a recipe for solving conflict.

item.title: Nepal’s Gen Z Uprising Probe: Shoddy Report, Hasty Action
item.description: There are suspicions that the probe committee investigating the violence during the two-day protests wanted to protect some people from prosecution.

item.title: Big Tech Shouldn’t Be Writing the Rules for AI
item.description: Anthropic’s clash with the Trump administration has exposed the extent to which governments have abdicated their responsibility over AI governance. To prevent powerful technologies from being shaped solely by corporate profit incentives, democracies must establish the institutions needed to oversee them.

item.title: Telling the Truth About China’s Success
item.description: With the Persian Gulf in flames, de-escalation of the cold war between the United States and China must become the world’s top priority. To that end, it is essential to explode a powerful myth: the idea that China has cheated its way to prosperity.

item.title: Why US-China Decoupling Isn’t Happening
item.description: Despite ongoing debates about Sino-American decoupling, the global economy remains as integrated as ever. Whenever either superpower tries to restrict ties with the other, it accelerates a broader process of adaptation and adjustment that is making the system more resilient and harder to control unilaterally.

item.title: MAGA Goes to War
item.description: For the past decade, Donald Trump has promised to put America first by keeping the country out of any more costly “forever wars”—a message that has carried him to the US presidency twice. Yet now the man who was only recently demanding a Nobel Peace Prize has emerged as a world-class warmonger.

item.title: Is Germany’s Postwar Consensus on Israel in Peril?
item.description: The political left has become simultaneously more mainstream and more radical.

item.description: How America and Israel can shape a new Middle East.

item.description: Despite the advantages it enjoys as a net oil exporter, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim said that the country cannot afford to get "too comfortable."

item.title: Southeast Asia Feels the Squeeze from Middle East War
item.description: Even the region's energy-rich countries are now being forced to reckon with the economic fallout from the Iran conflict.

item.description: However, prosecutors are appealing the five-year sentence handed to 22-year-old Fandi Ramadhan for his role in the trafficking of nearly two tonnes of methamphetamines.

item.description: Algiers has historically close ties with Tehran—but it may benefit from the ongoing war.

item.description: The Trump-Xi summit itself is likely to be less important than the divergent strategies that it may reveal.

item.description: It’s even more complicated than capturing a Venezuelan president.

item.description: For Iranians under fire, surges of patriotism and criticism of the government.

item.description: Economic stabilization achieved over the past fiscal year, when inflation finally began to decline, could prove painfully short-lived if the crisis does not end soon.

item.description: Iran’s new leaders are more radical and less risk-averse than their predecessors.

item.description: Shairbek, who resigned from parliament last month, had previously been questioned as a “witness" in the Kyrgyzneftgas case.

item.title: The Graveyard of Rohingya Diplomacy: Is Bangladesh Stuck in an ‘Exhaustion Trap’?
item.description: For almost a decade now, the gap between official progress and political reality has stalled Rohingya diplomacy.

item.title: Why Europe Is Unlikely to Face an Inflation Surge
item.description: In 2021-22, the European Central Bank failed to pay sufficient heed to mounting inflationary pressures, leading to a delayed response that made matters much worse. While today’s energy-price shock remains less acute than the one caused by Russia’s 2022 invasion of Ukraine, the ECB appears committed to a more proactive response.

item.title: Cobalt, Congo, and China: Battery Power as Political Power
item.description: Insights from Nicholas Niarchos.

item.description: With the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed, Bangladesh is in a dire strait of its own and looking far afield for new energy partners, like Kazakhstan.

item.title: How Will South Korea Respond to the Iran-US Conflict?
item.description: Amid U.S. requests for naval involvement in the Strait of Hormuz, Seoul has three options: leveraging, minimizing, or compensating.

item.title: Can China Grow From Within?
item.description: At a time of intensifying geopolitical volatility, China’s embrace of a consumption-led model is not only about rebalancing growth, but also about anchoring it more firmly at home. Domestic demand offers insulation from external shocks, and along with developed capital markets, it can go a long way toward strengthening autonomy.

item.title: What’s Next for Cuba?
item.description: From Venezuela to Iran, US President Donald Trump’s administration has apparently embraced a simple mantra: attack now, plan later. In Cuba’s case, the attack has so far taken the form not of airstrikes, but of blockade on fuel imports, which has produced a severe economic and humanitarian crisis. But while Trump speaks glibly of the Cuban regime’s “imminent demise” and a “friendly takeover” of the country, he has offered no vision for the island’s future, though he may have created a blueprint for China to apply in Taiwan.
